macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Laws of Guarantors (Arevim) and the Distinction Between Verbal and Kinyan Obligations

micro_segments.title: Opening: The Daf and the Locus Classicus of Guarantor Law
Two Foundational Questions About Guarantors: How Can a Guarantor Bind Himself Without a Kinyan, and How Is This Not an Asmachta?
The Gemara's Resolution: Guarantorship Is Binding Because It Relies on Trust and Integrity, Not Merely on Asmachta
The Mishna's Rule: Do Not Go to the Guarantor First; Go to the Debtor First

macro_segments.title: The Order of Collection from Debtor vs. Guarantor and the Conditions Under Which Each May Be Pursued

micro_segments.title: Mishna and Gemara Discussion: The Basic Rule and Rabbi Yochanan's Limitation
Reconstruction of the Mishna: Five Gradations of Guarantor Rights (Via Tosafot)
Level One: Basic Guarantor (Arev Stam) – Collection Only When Debtor Proves Fruitless
Level Two: Guarantor with Retained Right – Collection When Debtor Has No Obvious Assets (Karka)
Level Three: Guarantor When Debtor Has Obvious Property – Must Pursue Debtor First Even with Retained Right
Level Four: Kablan (One Who Says "Give Him and I Will Give") – Can Be Pursued First Regardless of Debtor's Assets
Level Five: Nasa V'Natan B'Yad (Money Given Directly to Guarantor, Who Hands It Over) – Only Guarantor Is Debtor
Rav Huna's Rule: The Distinction Between Arev and Kablan Based on Language ('Halvehu' vs. 'Ten Lo')
Debate Among Amoraim: Rav Yitzchak (Explicit Label Controls) vs. Rav Chisda (Default to Kablan) vs. Rava (Default to Arev)
Clarification: When Guarantor Uses Language of 'Ten Lo' (Give Him), Debtor Remains Obligated If Money Is Given Directly to Debtor

macro_segments.title: The Right of a Guarantor to Collect from the Debtor's Property as a Secondary Obligation and the Concept of Property as Guarantor

micro_segments.title: The Case of the Judge Who Seized Debtor's Property Without Giving Debtor Opportunity to Pay – Rav Chani's Reversal
Rava's Explanation: A Debtor's Property Serves as His Guarantor; Collection From Property Is Secondary to Direct Obligation
The Halakha: Must First Attempt Direct Collection From Debtor Before Seizing Property

macro_segments.title: The Right of a Guarantor Who Has Paid to Collect Reimbursement from the Debtor (or Debtor's Estate)

micro_segments.title: Case: Guarantor Paid Creditor on Behalf of Father; Orphans Cannot Yet Be Compelled – Rav Pappa's Position
Debate on Rav Pappa's Rationale: Two Interpretations – Orphans Underage vs. Debt Was Not Yet Actionable
Rav Huna bar Rav Yehoshua's Alternative: Concern That Loan Was Already Prepaid (Tzrari) – Not an Age Issue
Distinguishing Cases: When Guarantor CAN Collect from Orphans (Debtor Admitted Debt or Died in Niddui)
When Guarantor CANNOT Collect: Role of Written Acknowledgment (Hikbalti) and Conflict in Interpretations

macro_segments.title: Application of Guarantor Law to Non-Jewish Creditors and Conclusion of the Sugya

micro_segments.title: Case: Guarantor Paid Non-Jewish Creditor Before Pursuing Orphans – Can Guarantor Now Collect from Orphans?
Rav Mordechai's Position: Since Non-Jews Go Straight to Guarantor, No Concern for Tzrari; Guarantor Can Collect
Objection: Even with Non-Jews, We ARE Concerned About Tzrari (Prepayment); Guarantor Cannot Collect from Orphans
Final Halakha: Summary of Three Cases Where Law Differs from Rav Pappa (Non-Jew, Niddui, Admission) and Introduction to Document vs. Oral Loan

topical_tags.term: Rav Pappa (Underage Orphans Cannot Be Compelled)
Rav Huna bar Rav Yehoshua (Tzrari Concern)
